direct_disclosure_scan.description: Scanned all 19 message snippets across 5 conversations for direct naming of hidden conditions.

findings.details_hypertension: Mary never says 'blood pressure,' 'hypertension,' or any medical term. The closest language is: 'numbers were still a little high' (CONV-001, Jan 22), 'watch the sodium and come back in a few months' (CONV-001, Jan 22), 'checked my numbers at the walgreens machine and they were actually way better' (CONV-001, Mar 12). These are appropriately vague behavioral clues without clinical naming.
findings.details_shellfish: Mary never says 'allergy,' 'allergic,' 'shellfish allergy,' 'shellfish sensitivity,' or any medical terminology. The closest language is: 'last time somebody used the same fryer for everything and i was NOT about to risk it' (CONV-001, Jan 10), 'my stomach was NOT happy for like two days straight' (CONV-003, Feb 7), 'i'm gonna call ahead and ask about shared fryers' (CONV-003, Feb 7), 'I will make sure the baccala has no shrimp in the oil. Olive oil only' (CONV-004, Feb 14). All references describe behavior (avoidance, checking, bringing own food) rather than naming the condition.
findings.details_religion: Catholic identity is shown through behavior (fish fry volunteering, Ash Wednesday, Mass attendance, Lent observance) rather than declarations like 'I am Catholic.' The parish is referenced but never named.
findings.details_political: No political statements, party names, candidate references, or policy opinions appear in any conversation.

people_match.description: Cross-referenced all conversation participants against the social circle file's inner_circle list.
people_match.notes: All 5 inner circle members have dedicated conversations. No one outside the inner circle appears as a conversation participant. This is consistent with the social circle file's selection rationale.

needle_chain_verification.description: Verified chronological ordering and logical coherence of all three needle chains declared in messenger metadata.

details.content: Mary tells Lucia her numbers were 'a little high,' doctor wants her to watch sodium and recheck in 6 months. Lucia references Nonna's heart problems.
Mary asks Dana for low sodium recipes, mentions cutting back on salt, meal prepping more to avoid eating fries at work. Dana's mom had 'similar stuff.'
Mary tells Lucia her Walgreens numbers were 'way better than january.' Running and cutting back on salt is working. Lucia references Dad also not listening about salt.

hypertension.chronology_check: Jan 22 -> Feb 12 -> Mar 12. Correct temporal progression: initial diagnosis, dietary behavior change, improvement result. Logically coherent: each step builds on the previous.

details.content: Mary tells Lucia she's bringing her own pasta salad to parish fish fry. References 'last time somebody used the same fryer.' Lucia says 'Always ask twice.'
Nonna making baccala for Lent fish fry. Mary bringing own pasta because 'i can't eat half the stuff there.' Nonna ensures olive oil only, no shrimp in the oil. Nonna says 'You are like your mother.'
Mary asks Jess to check online menu for new Thai place. References 'that pasta incident last year where my stomach was NOT happy for like two days.' Plans to call ahead about shared fryers.

shellfish.chronology_check: Jan 10 -> Feb 7 -> Feb 14. Correct temporal order. Logical progression: general avoidance at community event, restaurant-specific checking behavior, family accommodation at Lenten event.

details.content: Jess tells Mary that Kaylee made a bad schedule. Mary vents about being passed over: 'i trained her jess. i've been here 2 years longer.' They swap shifts.
Mary asks Jess about ServSafe. New location opening in Sun Prairie needs supervisors. Mary says 'i am NOT getting passed over again.' ServSafe is $200.
Mary asks Tony about Madison College because she's looking at their hospitality program. Tony is excited and offers to show her around campus.

career.chronology_check: Jan 14 -> Feb 21 -> Mar 18. Correct temporal progression: frustration with current situation, concrete action plan (ServSafe), educational exploration (community college). Each step escalates Mary's career investment.

communication_style_consistency.description: Verified that each person's texting style matches the social circle profile descriptions.

Lucia.predicted_style: Practical, direct, slightly protective. Short authoritative maternal phrases. Texts in full sentences. Would double-text. Sends food photos and Mass reminders.
Lucia.actual_style_observed: Full sentences with proper capitalization. Signs off 'Love Mom' (4/4 snippets). Protective warnings ('Always ask twice,' 'Take care of yourself please'). Sends practical suggestions (low sodium cookbook). Worries visibly ('Are you OK??'). Uses periods and questions marks properly.

Dana.predicted_style: Warm but direct. Proactive (texts if Mary absent). Encouraging. Practical (SBA loan advice).
Dana.actual_style_observed: Uses exclamation points for encouragement ('YES!! Mary!!'). Proactively checks in ('Didn't see you at the lake this morning!'). Gives practical life advice (FAFSA, SBA loans). Uses proper capitalization and grammar. Calls Mary's bullet journal an 'empire.'

Jess.predicted_style: Casual work-friend register. Shift logistics, light complaints. Fun and spontaneous.
Jess.actual_style_observed: Lowercase, casual ('dude,' 'lmaooo,' 'literally'). Rapid back-and-forth exchanges. Work venting ('she's been on a whole power trip'). Makes plans spontaneously ('OPENING DAY IS IN TWO WEEKS'). Uses caps for excitement. Supportive ('you'd be an amazing supervisor').

Nonna.predicted_style: Minimal texting given age (80s). Simple and direct if texting at all. Relationship built on presence.
Nonna.actual_style_observed: Very short messages (1-2 sentences max). Slow response times (37-minute gaps). Imperatives ('Bring bread. The good kind'). No abbreviations or emoji. Directive tone. Signs off with 'Dont forget' and 'Love you too.'

Tony.predicted_style: Casual, abbreviated. Mary probably initiates more.
Tony.actual_style_observed: Short messages, often one line. Casual ('yeah probably,' 'lmao,' 'say less'). Mary initiates 3 of 4 conversations. Tony's responses come with delays (10:04 -> 10:30 is 26-minute gap). Supportive in his own way ('you're literally the hardest worker there').

interview_facts_in_messenger.notes: 14 major interview facts are reflected in messenger logs. 8 are missing. The missing items are mostly private hobbies (Sudoku, reading, music) that don't naturally come up in text conversations, or specific cultural references (Wes Anderson, Door County) that could appear in a longer message history. Garden appears briefly via Nonna. The most notable gap is Door County — given its importance to the Dana friendship, a planning text would be expected.

summary: The messenger logs are well-constructed. All 5 inner circle members are present with appropriate conversation volumes. No hidden facts are directly named — the forward process (indirect behavioral clues only) is correctly implemented. All 3 needle chains are chronologically correct and logically coherent. Conversation topics strongly match social circle predictions. Communication styles are distinctive and consistent with character profiles.
suggested_fixes: Consider adding a Door County trip planning snippet in CONV-002 (Dana), since this is described as a key shared activity in both the interview and social circle file but is absent from the messenger logs.
The 'art walks and museum exhibits' interest from the seed appears nowhere in messenger logs. A brief mention of a gallery opening or museum visit in any conversation would increase coverage.
Music preferences (John Prine, Bon Iver) have zero representation. Even a brief 'have you heard the new Bon Iver track' text from Dana would embed this preference naturally.
Nonna has only 3 snippets while all others have 4. Adding one more short exchange (e.g., Easter planning or a garden follow-up) would balance the distribution.
Consider adding one or two typos or autocorrect mistakes to Mary's messages for increased naturalism. Current texting is very clean for a fast-typing 28-year-old.
